tutorial.solution: 2140C - Ultimate Value Note that if Bob chooses two indices l , r l , r on his turn, then Alice can also choose the same indices l , r l , r on her next turn and this will increase the value of f ( a ) f ( a ) ultimately. So, it remains optimal for Bob to end the game on his first turn. Now there are two possibilities: Alice can end the game on her first turn, and Alice can choose two indices and Bob ends the game. For the first case, finding f ( a ) f ( a ) is trivial. Thus, we are left to find the maximum value of f ( a ) f ( a ) that Alice can attain on her first turn by choosing two indices. There are only two types of swaps between even index values and odd index values: We swap even with even index or odd with odd index. In this case, only cost increases by absolute difference in index. We swap even with odd or vice versa. In this case, the total change is | i − j | + 2 ⋅ ( a i − a j ) | i − j | + 2 ⋅ ( a i − a j ) where i mod 2 = 0 i mod 2 = 0 and j mod 2 = 1 j mod 2 = 1 . This can be solved by maintain prefix minimum over odd and even indexes. Time Complexity: O ( n ) O ( n )

tutorial.solution: First, calculate the total damage an entire magazine deals, which is sum of the array a a . We will have to use at least h / s u m h / s u m magazines. Now, the remaining health of the enemy would be h h modulo s u m s u m . If the remaining health is 0 0 , then we don't need to worry about the operation. If the remaining health is greater than 0 0 , then we will try to perform an operation that can minimise the number of bullets required in the last magazine. For every i i from 1 1 to n n , we will check if we can kill the enemy using only i i bullets from the last magazine. When checking for the index i i , the most optimal operation will always be to swap m i n ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) m i n ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) and m a x ( a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … a n ) m a x ( a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … a n ) . If m i n ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) > m a x ( a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … a n ) m i n ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) > m a x ( a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … a n ) , then it is optimal to not perform the operation for this i i . After performing the operation (or not performing), if s u m ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) s u m ( a 0 , a 1 , … a i ) becomes less than or equal to the remaining health, then it is possible to kill the enemy using only i i bullets from the last magazine. The smallest i i satisfying this condition gives us the most optimal answer. Time complexity: O ( n ) O ( n )

tutorial.solution: First of all, flipping a bit an even number of times is equivalent to not flipping the bit at all, and flipping a bit an odd number of times is equivalent to flipping the bit just one time. So, to make all the bits equal to 0 0 , we have to make sure we flip all the 0 0 bits an even number of times and all the 1 1 bits an odd number of times. One of the ways to achieve this is, if the count of 0 0 bits in the string is odd, then apply the operation on all the 0 0 bits. If the count of 0 0 bits in the string is even and the count of 1 1 bits in the string is also even, then apply the operation on all the 1 1 bits. If the count of 0 0 bits in the string is even and the count of 1 1 bits in the string is odd, then after applying any operation, the count of 0 0 bits will always stay even. But the length of the string in this case is odd which means we cannot make all the bits equal to 0 0 . Hence in this case the answer will be − 1 − 1 . Time complexity: O ( n ) O ( n )
hints: What is the condition for -1?
If the length of the string is odd and the count of 1 is odd, then and only then the answer will be -1.

tutorial.solution: 2140A - Shift Sort The minimum number of operations is equal to the number of misplaced 1 1 's that are in the prefix of the string where 0 0 's should be. Let c 0 c 0 be the total count of zeros in the string s s . In the sorted version, the first c 0 c 0 characters will be 0 0 's. The number of operations needed is simply the count of 1 1 's in the prefix s [ 1 … c 0 ] s [ 1 … c 0 ] . The number of 1 1 's in the prefix s [ 1 … c 0 ] s [ 1 … c 0 ] (misplaced 1 1 's) is equal to the number of 0 0 's in the suffix s [ c 0 + 1 … n ] s [ c 0 + 1 … n ] (misplaced 0 0 's). Let this count be M M . A single operation can fix one misplaced 1 1 and one misplaced 0 0 . Pick a misplaced 1 1 at index i i and a misplaced 0 0 at index k k . Pick any index j j between them ( i < j < k i < j < k ). The triplet of characters ( s i , s j , s k ) ( s i , s j , s k ) will be ( 1 , bit , 0 ) ( 1 , bit , 0 ) . A cyclic shift can always turn this into ( 0 , bit , 1 ) ( 0 , bit , 1 ) , correcting both s i s i and s k s k . If the triplet is ( 1 , 0 , 0 ) ( 1 , 0 , 0 ) , a left shift results in ( 0 , 0 , 1 ) ( 0 , 0 , 1 ) . If the triplet is ( 1 , 1 , 0 ) ( 1 , 1 , 0 ) , a right shift results in ( 0 , 1 , 1 ) ( 0 , 1 , 1 ) . If there is no bit between the misplaced 1 1 and 0 0 then there has to be a 1 1 to the right of misplaced 0 0 or 0 0 to the left of misplaced 1 1 ( n ≥ 3 n ≥ 3 ). We can choose this bit and perform a left shift or right shift respectively. Since each operation fixes one such pair, the answer is M M . Time Complexity: O ( n ) O ( n )

Once we reach the conclusion that 0 0 must be present in all partitions, we can make the same conclusion for 1 , 2 , … , m − 1 1 , 2 , … , m − 1 . That is, m m is the only possible score.

tutorial.solution: Make a a a rearrangement of b b means the frequency of all numbers present in array a a should be equal to the frequency of all numbers present in array b b . For this to be possible, the combined frequency of all numbers present in array a a and array b b should be even. If at least one number has combined frequency as odd, the answer will be − 1 − 1 . Now, if the answer is not − 1 − 1 , let's see how to perform the operations. Create a graph with n n nodes and n n directed edges from node a i a i to b i b i . The operation swap a i a i and b i b i means the direction of the i i -th edge is reversed in the graph. This means, for each edge any direction is possible. Hence, we can consider the graph as an undirected graph, which we need to convert to a directed graph. In other words, we need to decide the direction of all the edges. In the directed graph, the indegrees and outdegrees of a node i i actually mean the frequency of i i in array a a and array b b respectively. Our problem is to make the frequency of each number in array a a equal to array b b , which means to make the indegrees of each node equal to its outdegree. Since, the combined frequency of all numbers is even, it means the degree of each node in the undirected graph is even. This means the graph contains Eulerian cycles. Each connected component can be treated like a separate graph (because it is possible that our graph is not a single connected component), and find Eulerian cycle in all these graphs. Keep assigning direction to the edges in the direction used in the Eulerian cycle and hence accordingly perform the operations. Time complexity: O ( n ) O ( n )

339A.name: 339A - Helpful Maths
339A.statement:
Xenia the beginner mathematician is a third year student at elementary school. She is now learning the addition operation.
The teacher has written down the sum of multiple numbers. Pupils should calculate the sum. To make the calculation easier, the sum only contains numbers 1, 2 and 3. For example, she can't calculate sum 1+3+2+1 but she can calculate sums 1+1+2 and 3+3.

Let's say we have one odd coin o c o c and several even coins e c 1 , e c 2 , … , e c m e c 1 , e c 2 , … , e c m . We will sort the even coins in descending order: e c 1 ≥ e c 2 ≥ … e c m e c 1 ≥ e c 2 ≥ … e c m . In this case, for k = 1 … ( m + 1 ) k = 1 … ( m + 1 ) , we can construct the following answers: o c o c o c + e c 1 o c + e c 1 o c + e c 1 + e c 2 o c + e c 1 + e c 2 … … o c + e c 1 + e c 2 + ⋯ + e c m o c + e c 1 + e c 2 + ⋯ + e c m . But what to do if there are several odd coins? Let o c o c be the largest of the odd coins. We will perform the above actions for all k k from 1 1 to ( m + 1 ) ( m + 1 ) . For k = ( m + 2 ) k = ( m + 2 ) , we will use the following construction: First, we will put any two odd coins that are not the coin o c o c into the bag. Next, we will put the coin o c o c and all even coins except the smallest one e c m e c m . For k = ( m + 3 ) k = ( m + 3 ) , we will add the smallest even coin e c m e c m to the already constructed answer. For k = ( m + 4 ) k = ( m + 4 ) , we will add two more "unnecessary" odd coins at the beginning and again remove e c m e c m . For k = ( m + 5 ) k = ( m + 5 ) , we will add e c m e c m again, and so on. Note for k = n k = n : if you have an even number of odd coins in total, then regardless of the order of addition, the bag will be emptied in the end.

tutorial.solution: Let’s brute force check all substrings of length <= 3 and print the lexicographically smallest one that doesn’t appear as a substring in the input. We can guarantee that we will come across the answer due to the pigeonhole principle. There are at most n + n − 1 + n − 2 = 3 n − 3 n + n − 1 + n − 2 = 3 n − 3 possible substrings of length 3 or shorter in the input. There exist 26 + 26 2 + 26 3 = 18278 26 + 26 2 + 26 3 = 18278 total substrings of length 3 or shorter. It is impossible for the input to contain all 18278 18278 substrings, as 3 n − 3 < 18278 3 n − 3 < 18278 for n ≤ 1000 n ≤ 1000 . Final runtime looks something like O ( 18278 n ) O ( 18278 n ) or O ( n ) O ( n ) depending on how you implement substring checking.

tutorial.solution: Div2A 2098A - Vadim's Collection Our goal is to obtain the minimally possible string that satisfies the conditions of beauty. Therefore, we need to arrange the digits in order from the first to the last, each time choosing the minimally possible suitable digit. More formally: For the i i -th position, we need to place the smallest available digit that is not less than 10 − i 10 − i . After placing a digit, it becomes unavailable for further use. We repeat the process for all 10 10 positions. Why does this work? Notice that when we place a digit in the i i -th position, we have at least i i digits in the original number that are greater than or equal to 10 − i 10 − i , meaning that at most i − 1 i − 1 digits have been used from them earlier, and there is always some option available. Thus, at each step, it is beneficial to choose the smallest available suitable digit because the number can always be completed to the end, and choosing a larger digit would result in a larger number. Div2B 2098B - Sasha and the Apartment Purchase Note that for a fixed position, the optimal point is the median (or for an even number of elements, any point between the two medians). Proof: Observe that if we move the point between x x and x + 1 x + 1 , the answer changes by the difference in the number of elements to the left and right between x x and x + 1 x + 1 . Therefore, f ( x ) f ( x ) monotonically decreases to the median. This means that the optimum is indeed located at the median. Now the problem has been reduced to the following: how many such points exist such that if we remove no more than k k of the original elements, this point remains the median. Note that to obtain the optimal answer, we can simply remove elements from the beginning or from the end, since if two elements are on different sides of the median, this action does nothing. Thus, our answer lies within the segment, and it is (the formulas need to be adjusted with the correct plus or minus one, we will leave this as an exercise for the participants) a n + k 2 − a n − k 2 a n + k 2 − a n − k 2 , where a a is sorted beforehand. Div1A 2097A - Sports Betting Let's sort the array a a in non-decreasing order: a 1 ≤ a 2 ≤ … ≤ a n a 1 ≤ a 2 ≤ … ≤ a n . Suppose Vadim argues with the students in turn. We can formalize the problem as follows: since there are only two possible seating arrangements each day, we can encode them with the digits 0 0 and 1 1 . Each of Vadim's predictions can be represented by a pair of numbers ( c i , d i ) ( c i , d i ) —the seating arrangement for days a i + 1 a i + 1 and a i + 2 a i + 2 , respectively. Vadim will be able to win in the following cases: If there exists a quadruple of students a i = a i + 1 = a i + 2 = a i + 3 a i = a i + 1 = a i + 2 = a i + 3 , meaning those students he will argue with on the same day. In this case, he can provide all possible 4 4 predictions for days a i + 1 a i + 1 and a i + 2 a i + 2 and will guarantee at least one correct prediction. If there exists a pair of indices i < j i < j such that a i = a i + 1 < a j = a j + 1 a i = a i + 1 < a j = a j + 1 and for each x ∈ { a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … , a j − 1 } x ∈ { a i + 1 , a i + 2 , … , a j − 1 } there exists a k k such that a k = x a k = x . Let x x be the color with the highest number of candies. In case of ties, x x could be any of the candidates. Denote the number of candies with color x x as a a . Conclusion: The answer to the problem is YES if and only if there are no more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x ; — in other words, a ≤ n 2 − n a ≤ n 2 − n holds. Proof of necessity: If there are more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x , then there are less than n n candies not of color x x . Since there are n n rows, at least one row will have no candies not of color x x ; therefore, such a row will be filled with candies of color x x , violating the restrictions. Proof of sufficiency: If there are no more than n 2 − n n 2 − n candies of color x x , we can always construct a solution with the method below. First, if a < n a < n holds, then we can arrange the candies arbitrarily because no color has enough candies to fill a row or a column. Otherwise, n ≤ a ≤ n 2 − n n ≤ a ≤ n 2 − n holds. We can put candies of color x x in a 1 , 1 , a 2 , 2 , … , a n , n a 1 , 1 , a 2 , 2 , … , a n , n . This will take n n candies of color x x . Next, we choose n n candies not of color x x arbitrarily. We will put them at a 1 , 2 , a 2 , 3 , … , a n − 1 , n , a n , 1 a 1 , 2 , a 2 , 3 , … , a n − 1 , n , a n , 1 . Lastly, we arrange other candies that have not yet been arranged arbitrarily. Since in the first step we put a candy of color x x in each row and column, and in the second step we put a candy not of color x x in each row and column, each row and column contains at least candies of two colors, satisfying the constraints.

4A.name: 4A - Watermelon
4A.statement:
One hot summer day Pete and his friend Billy decided to buy a watermelon. They chose the biggest and the ripest one, in their opinion. After that the watermelon was weighed, and the scales showed

Pete and Billy are great fans of even numbers, that's why they want to divide the watermelon in such a way that each of the two parts weighs even number of kilos, at the same time it is not obligatory that the parts are equal.
